steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence structure is: "a single tree like ___ claims dendrochronologist Valerie Trouet, can tell the history of the world." The phrase "claims dendrochronologist Valerie Trouet" is an interrupting attribution set off by a comma after it. The blank must provide the opening punctuation that matches — a comma after "this" to symmetrically set off the attribution phrase from the main clause.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — this: No punctuation leaves the attribution phrase unset off from the subject, making the sentence structurally ambiguous. ✗
  • Choice B — this,: A comma after "this" opens the interrupting attribution phrase "claims dendrochronologist Valerie Trouet," which is already closed by its own comma. The main clause reads cleanly: "a single tree like this … can tell the history of the world." ✓
  • Choice C — this:: A colon signals elaboration or a list — not the right relationship for an interrupting attribution phrase. ✗
  • Choice D — this;: A semicolon joins independent clauses. The phrase "claims dendrochronologist Valerie Trouet" is not an independent clause. ✗
B is correct.
